Fund performance

Institutional Class shares of Columbia Commodity Strategy Fund (MUTF:CCSZX) returned -0.21% for the fourth quarter ending December 31, 2024. The fund’s benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index, returned -0.45% for the same period.

Market overview

For the fourth quarter, the Bloomberg Commodities Index slid a marginal 0.45% following lackluster global demand for oil and a marginal decline in gold prices. China's persistent economic slowdown, characterized by sluggish construction, manufacturing and consumer activity, dampened oil consumption. Additionally, gold fell as a stronger dollar pushed the price per bullion lower.

Energy and livestock were the best performing components of the Index, while industrial metals and precious metals both declined in the quarter. Within energy, all sub- components achieved robust gains in the quarter. Within the agriculture component, unusual weather patterns, such as drought and high temperatures impacted agricultural output in many regions. Resulting in dramatic price movements in the quarter. Coffee and cocoa (not in the index) prices were significantly higher, while cotton and sugar prices were sharply lower. In industrial metals, all sub-components declined, with the biggest price declines seen in nickel and copper. In precious metals, gold and silver prices both fell in the quarter.

The U.S. stock market gained 2.75% in the fourth quarter, based on the large-cap Russell 1000 Index, bringing its return for the full year to 24.51%. The November election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developed-market peers. These factors helped propel stocks to a positive return despite indications that the U.S. Federal Reserve is unlikely to cut interest rates significantly in the year ahead.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

The Columbia Commodity Strategy Fund’s institutional share class returned -0.21% during the fourth quarter. The strategy outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Commodity Index which returned -0.45% over the same period. The fund’s outperformance against the benchmark can be attributed to sector allocation. Overweight positioning in energy was the primary driver of performance. Overweight allocations to softs and livestock also contributed to relative performance.

Slightly offsetting the above results was the fund’s security selection. Curve positioning in energy, grains and softs detracted from relative performance.

Additional details regarding the fund’s relative contributing and detracting sector exposures compared to the Bloomberg Commodity Index have been noted below:

Softs

  • Softs was the single largest contributing sector for the fund relative to the Bloomberg Commodity Index, driven primarily by an off-benchmark allocation to cocoa.
  • Coffee, cotton and sugar detracted from performance primarily driven from the fund's overweight position in sugar.

Industrial metals

  • Within industrial metals, security selection drove relative performance, led by curve positioning in copper and nickel.
  • Slightly offsetting these results were overweight allocations to zinc and lead.

Grains

  • The fund's curve positioning and sector allocation detracted from relative performance. This was led by the fund's curve positioning in corn. Overweight allocations to soybean meal and soybeans also detracted.
  • Slightly offsetting these results was curve positioning in soybean meal.

Energy

  • Negative results in security selection were the primary drivers to relative underperformance, while an overweight allocation to energy only marginally offset these results.
  • Curve positioning in natural gas and crude oil were the main detractors.
  • Marginally offsetting these results were overweight allocations to crude oil and natural gas.

Precious metals

  • Positive results were driven by curve positioning in both gold and silver versus the benchmark.

Livestock

  • Within livestock, overweight allocations to feeder cattle and lean hogs were the primary contributors to relative performance.
  • The fund's curve positioning in live cattle and lean hogs also aided performance relative to the Bloomberg Commodity Index.

Outlook

Heading into the first quarter of 2025, the primary themes we are paying attention to are weather, geopolitical risks and the uncertainty of Trump’s implementation of his fiscal policies, specifically tariffs.

As we begin a new year our expectation is that weather will continue to drive volatility in the commodity markets as the winter season brings near freezing temperatures boosting demand for heating products.

Geopolitical risk: the U.S. and UK imposed additional sanctions on Russia’s oil tankers, adding approximately 180 vessels to the sanctions list, bringing the total amount to more than 200 vessels. Most of this supply is going to countries who are now indicating compliance with the U.S. sanctions. This will result in lower crude supply and higher

prices. For Russia, the path forward is to either acquire non-sanctioned tankers to transport its oil or offer it at the imposed $60 price cap.

Lastly, potential Trump tariffs also threaten higher prices. Our team will continue to monitor these developments and more as we move into the new year.

data.title: Boeing Surprise: More Airplane Deliveries Compared To Airbus
data.description:
In January, I discussed the net orders and deliveries for Boeing (NYSE:BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) in 2025. Based on reader feedback, I am bringing the series back on a monthly basis. The aim of the series is to assess the recovery of Boeing relative to Airbus. This allows us to provide context on how much of the recovery is driven by improvements at Boeing. Secondly, it allows us to assess how much of the recovery is attributable to improvements of the aerospace supply chain.

Explanation Of Analysis Methodology

For this analysis, I will be using an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value. The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently we are not viewing things through a scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries is what matters and that is also the area where we see that Boeing nor Airbus have been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view on how the recovery for both manufacturers is tracking.

Airbus Books More Airplane In Orders Than Boeing In January

The table above shows the net orders by units as well as dollar value for both jet makers. I have also included a column marking the “winner”, but I do note that a single month does not provide a trend indicator. While I have a winner column in the table, I am not so much interested to view things through a lens of who is winning and who is losing. At least not at this point.

The table indicates that Boeing booked more single aisle orders in January while Airbus booked more wide body orders. In total, Airbus booked 15 more orders than Boeing in January and that also translated to the dollar value which was $3.4 billion higher.

Airbus And Boeing Book $7.6 Billion In Airplane Orders

Boeing and Airbus logged a total of 87 airplane orders in January. Airbus took a 59% share in the orders with the remaining 41% going to Boeing. Year-on-year, the net orders increased by 56 airplanes with both manufacturers logging more orders. In terms of value, the increase was $2.6 billion or 52%. The increase was driven by Boeing, which logged a net increase of $2.2 billion in order value. What I do note is that the order value stood at a multi-year high. Only in January 2020, the order value was higher. Driven by a more favorable mix, Airbus took 72% of the order value leaving 28% for Boeing.

Boeing Delivers More Airplanes In January

The reason why I take a data driven approach on things is because it provides a measurable basis. Currently, the perception is that Airbus is doing much better than Boeing. However, for January the figures show otherwise. This of course just one month of deliveries, which provides no indication of what the full year be like.

Boeing deliveries were higher than usual due to the fact that driven by the timing of the employee strike and work resumption, it could not deliver all airplanes in inventory in December. Usually, we see a bump in deliveries in December and a weaker January delivery volume due to this phenomenon.

Airbus, on the other hand, is facing a shortage of engines which will keep airplane deliveries lower in the first quarter of the year. The European jet maker is balancing getting grounded P&W powered airplanes back in the air while also having a flow of engine deliveries to support new engine deliveries. Furthermore, Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF) which provides the CFM LEAP 1A turbofan is recovering from production issues including hurricane damage. So, besides providing the data we also need to put things in context. The context shows different dynamics for both jet makers.

Driven by the higher delivery volumes, Boeing also saw its delivery surpass that of Airbus by $1.3 billion. This brought the delivery value to $2.8 billion for Boeing and $1.5 billion for Airbus.

Airbus And Boeing Book $7.6 Billion In Airplane Orders

Boeing and Airbus logged a total of 70 airplane orders in January. Airbus took a 36% share in the orders with the remaining 64% going to Boeing. Year-on-year, the net orders increased by 13 airplanes. Boeing delivered saw delivery volumes grow 18 units booking a multi-year high for January delivery numbers. Airbus deliveries decreased by five units. The delivery numbers show that Airbus has been alternating higher and lower deliveries over the past years and January 2025 was again a low year.

The increase in dollar value of deliveries was $1.1 billion or 33%. The increase was driven by Boeing, which logged a net increase of $1.2 billion. Airbus delivery value partially offset the total growth in delivery value. Boeing accounted for 65% of the value. The remaining 35% went to Airbus. Delivery value for Boeing was at the highest point since 2019. Total delivery value was at the highest point since 2022. Combined delivery value is still 28% below the highs of 2019. This shows a significant upward potential on delivery rates. Boeing only lagged $0.7 billion compared to 2019. Airbus was $1.1 billion behind. It shows that challenges on deliveries persist on both sides.

Conclusion: A Better Year Expected For Both Jet Makers

Boeing and Airbus saw higher net order inflow in January. Currently, I am not interested in the number of net orders. Both jet makers have backlogs of thousands of airplanes. What matters at this point is the number of deliveries. Airplane deliveries stood at a multi-year high. Timing of Boeing’s airplane deliveries were the reason for the increase in deliveries. The increase was partially offset by lower deliveries for Airbus.

The delivery flow in Q1 will not be smooth. However, as we progress throughout the year, we should be able to see robust growth. Continuous assessment of the deliveries provides a sense on where both jet makers stand in their attempt to increase production.

I mark both stocks a buy. Boeing is on a trajectory to improve production quality and output. Airbus, on the other hand, is aligning its supply chain with its production capacity. Both companies are facing different challenges, but the overall picture is a positive one for higher output.

data.title: PFFV: Preferred Shares Index ETF, Above-Average Yield And Performance Track-Record
data.description:
The Global X Variable Rate Preferred ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFV) invests in variable rate preferred shares, sports an above-average 7.2% dividend yield, below-average credit risk and duration. It is a strong investment opportunity, and a buy.

PFFV - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

PFFV invests in variable rate preferred shares. Preferreds are something of a hybrid asset class, with characteristics of both equities and bonds. In PFFV's case, these investments behave much more similarly to bonds than to equities, with above-average dividends and little possibility of substantial capital appreciation, under most conditions at least.

PFFV itself is an index ETF, tracking the ICE U.S. Variable Rate preferred Securities Index. Said index includes most variable rate preferreds subject to a basic set of inclusion criteria. It is a market-cap weighted index, with issuer caps meant to prevent excessive concentration.

The fund itself is much less diversified than average, with only 53 different investments, almost entirely banks. Banks tend to issue preferreds much more so than average, partly due to greater financial acumen, but mostly due to favorable (or less unfavorable) regulations. Banks need to meet certain capital requirements, tightened since the financial crisis, and preferreds help them meet these, hence their overweight position in PFFV and similar funds.

PFFV's overweight financials position increases risk, volatility, and exposes investors to significant losses and underperformance due to industry weakness. This has yet to be an issue for PFFV, but it has been an issue for some of its older peers. As an example, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), the largest ETF in this space, saw +60% drawdowns during the financial crisis, vastly exceeding high-yield bond losses. The financial crisis was concentrated in the banking sector, hence the fund's significant losses.

Although another recession centered on the banking industry seems unlikely, if only because we have no reason to expect one, it is definitely possible, and a risk for PFFV. More diversified funds don't suffer from these issues. Due to this, I wouldn't overweight PFFV in my portfolio or use it as a core portfolio holding, to reduce risk.

Rate Risk

PFFV invests in variable rate preferreds, which function a bit differently than other variable rate investments. These preferreds are generally fixed-to-variable, paying a fixed rate for an initial period of time, variable rate thereafter. As an example, one of PFFV's investment is a Bank of America preferred issued in 2015, with a 6.1% fixed rate until 2025, variable rate of 3m SOFR plus 4.15% thereafter. Specific preferred is as follows, you might have to squint a bit to read the table:

As should be clear from the example above, some of these variable preferreds have fixed rates for a very long amount of time, so, in practice, behave as fixed-rate securities. PFFV does not provide specific figures and, due to the complexities of these securities, it is not possible for me to estimate these either. Still, PFFV has some rate risk, even though it is nominally a variable rate fund.

PFFV saw losses of 13.8% during 2022, when the Fed started to hike rates, in-line with broader bond index ETFs / other bond sub-asset classes. Duration thus seems about average, of around 5 - 7 years.

PFFV's average duration does not significantly alter the fund's investment thesis, or lack thereof, nor is it an important advantage or disadvantage relative to peers. It is an important, and surprising, consideration for investors though. Dovish investors might prefer funds with higher duration, hawkish / risk-averse in those with zero. Lots will be surprised about a variable rate ETF having a moderate amount of rate risk, instead of close to zero.

Credit Risk

PFFV focuses on BBB-BB investments, basically in the middle of the pack.

Credit quality and risk are both moderate, higher than that of investment-grade funds, lower than that of high-yield funds. With inception in mid-2020, PFFV has yet to experience a downturn or recession, so we can't really gauge its performance during one. I would expect some losses during one, although far from significant ones.

Overall Volatility

PFFV's moderate rate and credit risk should result in moderate volatility, as seems to be the case. There are periods of above-average volatility though, including during most of 2025.

Dividend Yield Analysis

PFFV sports a 7.2% dividend yield, moderately higher than that of most bonds, slightly higher than most high-yield bonds.

It is a good yield on an absolute basis, and quite good for a fund with PFFV's credit risk. Remember, the fund invests quite heavily in BBB preferreds, and very little in those below BB. Quality is moderately higher than the average high-yield bond ETF, dividend yields marginally higher.

Most variable rate ETFs have seen their dividend yields increase almost in lock-step with Fed rates and so sport above-average yields because Fed rates have risen. I'm not sure that this is the case for PFFV, as most of the fund's preferreds are fixed-to-variable. Comparing the fund's dividend yield with that of the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) shows that PFFV's yield has risen by less than expected for a variable rate ETF too.

In my opinion, and considering the above, PFFV's strong, above-average yield is not entirely due to Fed hikes and so should prove somewhat resilient in the face of cuts. In the very long-term cuts should lead to lower dividend yields, but it could take over a decade for these to fully materialize.

In any case, PFFV's above-average yield is an important benefit for shareholders, especially considering the fund's risk profile. Income is above-average, risk just about average, a good combination.

Performance Track-Record

PFFV's performance track-record is reasonably good, with the fund outperforming most bonds and bond sub-asset classes since inception, matching the performance of high-yield corporate bonds. Relative to these, the fund outperformed prior to 2022, underperformed since. Some combination of greater exposure to rates / spreads seems likely to explain these results, although I'm not confident in this. Returns seem effectively identical since around 2023.

PFFV's performance track-record should have been materially stronger if the fund had experienced the entirety of the pandemic and had seen lower losses than high-yield bonds during it (consistent with credit ratings). In that case, we would have a fund with comparable returns to high-yield bonds, slightly higher yields, slightly lower drawdowns. It would be a potent combination, if not an outstanding one.

We don't have the data though, which is an issue here. For me, the credit quality data we do have is more than enough, for others perhaps not. The performance information we do have is reasonably good too, regardless.

Conclusion

PFFV's above-average 7.2% dividend yield, below-average credit risk and reasonably good performance track-record make it a buy.

data.title: Texas Instruments: Investors Should Be Cautious As The Valuation Is Detached From Reality
data.description:
Thesis

Despite the company's struggles, Texas Instruments Inc. (NASDAQ:TXN) stock has gained over 25% in the past year. In the below analysis I find that the technicals are very strong currently but the fundamentals show a different story. While Q4 still had negative revenue and EPS growth, there was significant improvement over mid 2024 results. However, guidance shows that the recovery is on hold as the declines in these financials are expected to reaccelerate in Q1. While that is the case, both the P/S and P/E ratios near three year highs leading me to believe that the stock is materially overvalued currently. Therefore, since the technicals and the fundamentals clash on the stock's outlook, I believe a hold rating would be most appropriate.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

TXN stock was in a sharp downtrend from early November to late December but the stock broke out. Even though some declines still occurred after the breakout, the rapid declines seen in the downtrend seem to be over. In recent days, the stock has soared with multiple gaps, confirming the end of the downtrend. The increase in volume in recent days helps to show the validity of this surge. It is too early to conclude that TXN is now in an uptrend but nonetheless, the stock is in a good position.

Support

The former downtrend line could have acted as support but it has moved far out of range. Currently, the nearest source of support is at around the 200 level. This level acted as support for periods in October and November while being key resistance in January. The next support level is an upside gap at around 196. Even though the doji are quite close together, the fact that this level was major support in September and November, and that the volume on the day of the gap was very high, its importance is not to be doubted. Lastly, there is support at 186 as there was another upside gap there. The volume on that day was high as well and this level was support at the beginning of January.

Resistance

There is relatively little resistance for TXN stock in this timeframe. The only major resistance would be at around 211. This level was major resistance in late September and late October and so if the stock continues to rally, this area could potentially act as a ceiling.

Takeaway

I would argue that the chart is looking bullish for TXN. The stock broke out of the sharp downtrend and has recently surged. There is also much more support below the stock than there is resistance above and so investors should find this price action to be encouraging.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average ('SMA')

The 50 day SMA had a death cross with the 200 day SMA back in January. The gap between the SMAs expanded to a great degree, showing accelerating long term bearish momentum. However, in recent days the 50 day SMA's trajectory has turned positive and so bearishness may be starting to recede. With the stock now above both SMAs, expect the gap between the lines to close.

Exponential Moving Average ('EMA')

The 9 day EMA just had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA, showing that there has been near term strength in TXN stock. Given the extremely sharp trajectory of the 9 day EMA, expect the gap between these EMAs to expand rapidly to reflect the quickly accelerating near term bullish momentum.

Bollinger Bands

In recent days, the stock soared above the Bollinger Bands' midline as a bullish signal. This helped to provide confirmation that another downtrend is not forming. Instead, this breakout could foreshadow that the stock is entering into a new uptrend. Currently, the stock is far above the upper band and so it is likely significantly overbought. Therefore an overbought correction is likely in the near future.

Takeaway

I believe the moving averages as a whole are quite bullish for TXN stock. The SMAs are still in an overall bearish position but they show that longer term bearishness could be starting to recede. The EMAs reflect the rapidly increasing near term bullishness and lastly, the stock's breakout above the Bollinger Bands' midline is a significant signal with multiple positive implications.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence ('MACD')

The MACD just had a bullish crossover with its signal line in recent days. The MACD line has soared and so the gap between the lines has expanded rapidly, as demonstrated by the very strong histogram. This indicates that bullish momentum is soaring.

Relative Strength Index ('RSI')

The RSI is currently at 69.39, very close to entering the 70+ zone. This indicator has soared past the 50 level in recent days showing that there has been a major change in sentiment as the bulls are now in solid control of the stock. The RSI is currently at six month highs despite the stock being far below its six month high, showing that there is extraordinary strength building in TXN stock.

Stochastics

From late January to mid February, there were multiple crossovers in the stochastics that ended up being false signals with both lines remaining in the 20 zone. A true bullish crossover occurred recently as the %K pulled away from the %D. Both lines have soared past the 50 level and the %K has entered into the 80+ zone, further evidence that the bulls are now in firm control.

Takeaway

In my view, there is no doubt that these indicators are bullish for TXN stock. The MACD just had a bullish crossover and shows accelerating momentum, the RSI is at its highest level in six months, and the stochastics displayed many promising indications as of late.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

TXN released their 2024 Q4 earnings in late January and showed relatively respectable results overall. They reported revenues of $4.01 billion, down 2% YoY and an EPS of $1.30, down 13% YoY. As you can see in the above chart, while revenue growth is negative, it represents great improvement since mid 2024 and is actually at around average levels in relation to the past three years. It is a similar story for EPS growth as its current negative growth still represents strong improvement from mid 2024 levels. EPS growth remains at below average levels compared to the past few years. Both revenue and GAAP EPS also showed strong performance versus consensus estimates as they beat by $134.57 million and $0.10 respectively. A highlight in their earnings would be free cash flow growth. They reported $1.498 billion FCF in their Q4 versus $1.349 billion in the prior year period, showing that growth was 11%. Overall, Q4 results show some recovery in the business, as there were significant improvements in key metrics.

Guidance

TXN provided guidance for their 2025 Q1. They are expecting revenues to range from $3.74 billion to $4.06 billion. This implies growth of between -6.7% and +1.2% and so at the midpoint, it would be a worsening of YoY revenue declines compared to Q4. They also provided EPS guidance of between $0.94 and $1.16. Therefore, EPS declines are expected to be in range of 10.8% to 27.7%. At the midpoint, this would show that YoY EPS declines are expected to worsen as well. After a promising Q4 quarter, I believe this guidance dents confidence as the recovery in TXN may be on hold according to these projections.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at 11.98 and is at far above average levels compared to the past three years. I believe this ratio shows overvaluation in TXN stock. As discussed above, revenue growth has recovered significantly and is at average levels relative to the past few years. However, guidance shows that this recovery trend will not continue in Q1 as they expect declines in revenue to worsen modestly. These financial results and projections are unable to justify a P/S ratio that is far above average and near three year highs. This shows there is a significant disconnect between the valuation and fundamentals. Currently, the IT sector's median P/S ratio is only 3.32 and so TXN is at an over 260% premium to peers. Its negative growth cannot justify this rich of a premium to the sector in my view.

Price To Earnings Ratio (P/E)

It is a very similar story for the P/E ratio. It is currently at 39.3, far above the three year average and is in fact very near three year highs as well. I also believe it shows overvaluation in TXN stock. As discussed above, while EPS declines have moderated in Q4, overall growth is still below average. In addition, Q1 guidance shows that the declines are expected to worsen again. While this is the case, the P/E ratio nears multi year highs. This is a major disconnect that shows the valuation is detached from the financial results of the company. The IT sector's median GAAP P/E ratio is currently 31.71 and so the stock is also at a premium to peers according to this measure.

Takeaway

I conclude that TXN is significantly overvalued at current levels. Both the P/S and P/E ratios show significant disconnect with their respective financial growth figures. Both ratios are also at a premium to the sector while guidance shows that declines in revenue and EPS are expected to reaccelerate.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are very strong for TXN stock. However, the fundamentals show a different story. As discussed above, while Q4 results were respectable and showed improvement, guidance was weak and shows a re-acceleration in the declines in revenue and EPS. Furthermore, the valuation is currently detached from reality as the near multiyear highs in the P/S and P/E ratios cannot be justified by the state of the financials. Therefore, the technical analysis and fundamental analysis are highly split on their outlook for TXN stock and as a result I initiate TXN at a hold rating.

data.title: Republic Services: A Buy-And-Hold Forever? Maybe Not At This Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Republic Services (NYSE:RSG) is a wonderful business and one which boasts a wide and robust economic moat. The firm enjoys multiple competitive advantages within the waste management industry and is the second largest player behind Waste Management (WM).

The company accordingly has a tangible degree of earnings power with 2024 being one of their most successful years to date.

These factors combined with a mature and stable industry are traditionally present in companies I would ideally like to hold forever, ceteris paribus of course.

However, my DCF suggests the company may be trading at up to an 80% overvaluation which implies a very rich valuation is currently present in shares.

As a result, and despite my utmost admiration of this wonderful business, I must award the stock a Hold at present time due to what I consider to be an unsustainable valuation.

Business Overview & Economic Moat

Republic Services is one of a few key players in the waste or garbage industry alongside big-brother Waste Management (who are about twice the size or Republic) and little-brother Waste Connections (WCN) (about half the size of Republic).

Like its competitors, the firm’s operations boil down to three key functions: collection, transportation and processing.

Republic operates about 17,000 trucks across the country and annually services around 13 million customers. This fleet of garbage trucks illustrates the scale at which Republic operates with replication of such assets being difficult due to the associated demand, capital and operational requirements.

Collection itself accounts for about 70% of revenues with the firm holding multiple lucrative franchise agreements with municipalities (such as Fort Collins, CO as an example) and commercial customers.

These contracts provide Republic Services with exclusive waste collection rights. Such contracts are also traditionally indexed to consumer price inflation helping to keep real earnings for the company stable.

While I doubt that these franchise deals allow for an increased premium to be charged by Republic for their services (as such agreements are usually struck through public tender offerings), I like the surety of income they generate with 80% of Republic's total order book being based on such annuity-style contracts.

Municipality deals also allow Republic to massively increase their route density which means that the firm collects more trash per distance driven thus increasing efficiency.

After collection, Republic transports the waste to either landfills where it is deposited or transfer stations where garbage can be compacted and placed into long-distance haulers.

Republic’s 250-odd transfer stations allow the firm to both move excess waste to processing facilities further away from collection zones while simultaneously allowing third-parties to do so as well, for a fee. The transfer business accounts for about 12% of revenues.

Finally, the waste processing operations at Republic Service consist of 208 active landfills, 75 recycling centres and 23 treatment, storage and disposal facilities (these are used for special, harmful waste classes). Landfill’s account for 12% of revenues for the firm while their other processing activities generate about 11%.

Landfills have decreased in numbers by about 90% since the late 1980s due to the strict environmental regulations placed on such facilities as well as societal displeasure of having sites near towns.

As a result, the largest operators (WM, RSG and WCN) have been basically the only ones with the resources and capabilities to satisfy environmental requirements and regulation thus generating significant pricing power for the associated services.

Republic allows other third-party garbage operators to utilise their sites in exchange for what are often dubbed “tipping fees”. A similar situation is present at the firm’s many recycling and processing facilities which help generate additional revenues for the company.

When considering the moat these operations may provide Republic Services, I think it is clear that the firm benefits from tangible network effects, cost advantages and an irreplaceable set of assets. The significant route density afforded by franchise contracts helps to increase Republic’s market share and network effects while their massive economies of scale decrease the proportional expense on fixed costs.

Equally, Republic Service's landfills are simply irreplaceable yet essential and thus affords the firm massive pricing power.

These factors combine to create significant barriers to entry and moat which protect the firm’s massive returns from erosive competition.

Fiscal Analysis

On the topic of returns, Republic Services has been steadily out earning their WACC of about 7.5% with the firm generating an annualised five-year average ROIC of 9.2%.

While this margin is quite slim, the firm’s essential services combined with a competitive industry position should continue to allow Republic to charge rates which allow it to generate a healthy return from operations.

The firm has also produced five-year average ROA and ROE of about 6% and 16% respectively which illustrates just how well the firm is able to produce value from their operations.

Margins are also quite healthy considering the relative competition Republic Services faces from both Waste Management, Waste Connections and smaller players within the industry. Five-year gross, operating and net margins of 40%, 18% and 10% respectively are almost identical to those earned by WM and consistently about 1-3pp ahead of WCN.

These broad profitability and return ratios support my hypothesis that the firm has real pricing power and that their moat effectively protects the company's operations from competition.

The latest annual report produced by Republic Services just one week ago highlighted the quality of earnings generated by the company in 2024.

Strong pricing power (a 6.5% increase YoY) combined with a slight increase in volumes allowed Republic Services to grow revenues by 7.1% YoY to $16 billion. The firm’s continued strategy of charging extra for different types of trash has allowed the company to continue growing earnings while ensuring that their core service offering remains competitively priced.

Republic’s lucrative franchise contracts and ability to charge third-party operators tipping and transfer fees also allowed the company to increase landfill, transfer and environmental solutions revenues in 2024 which all collectively contributed to the wonderful top-line growth.

Most impressively, Republic also managed to decrease the size of operating costs as a proportion of revenues from about 60% in 2023 to just 58% in 2024. This decrease came thanks to the additional revenues and continued focus management applies to ensuring costs associated with their core operations remain at a minimum.

Operating expenses were also essentially flat YoY which allowed operating income to increase a whopping 15% YoY to $3.2 billion. Assuming an effective tax-rate of about 21%, Republic Services was left with a NOPAT at the end of 2024 of around $2.5 billion.

Simply put, Republic Services continues to excel in their operations with the firm extracting record profitability from their business. Seeking Alpha’s Quant "A" Profitability rating supports this thesis.

Management has also provided guidance for 2025 consisting of revenue growth around 5% YoY as well as an operating margin of approximately 30%. I suspect the company will be able to meet these targets with almost certainty bar the effects any sudden or unforeseen macroeconomic shock could have.

Moving over to the balance sheet I am happy to report that Republic’s economic underpinnings are sustainable.

The firm has total current assets of $2.4 billion which includes about $74 million in cash while total current liabilities amount to a sizeable $4.1 billion. The imbalance leaves the company with a current ratio of only 0.58x which is to be expected given the rate at which Republic is able to generate cash.

Republic also has about $11.9 billion in both long-term debentures and equity which leaves the firm with a debt/equity ratio of approximately 1x. Given how stable and mature the waste industry is (both in terms of technology and market share), I find these levels elevated yet fair and consider the firm to be employing capital effectively especially considering the spread out debt profile.

I suspect the firm will continue to maintain their leverage levels around their current level of 2.8x with the firm planning to spend another $1 billion on acquisitions in 2025. Acquiring smaller waste companies is Republic’s primary growth strategy and given their proven ability to integrate these new operations into their network, I view it as an effective one.

Valuation

I always like to begin my valuation analysis using Seeking Alpha’s wonderful Valuation tab. For RSG, the Quant has provided a “D-“ letter grade based on how the firm’s metrics compare to those of other businesses in the industrial sector.

Much like the quant, I believe the current P/E GAAP TTM of 35x is excessive even for a firm as profitable and stable as Republic Services as the underlying YoY earnings growth rate of about 7% is unsupportive of such an elevated ratio.

The P/S TTM ratio of 4.5x is also very high for a firm that has historically grown revenues at about 5% per year and illustrates the premium (essentially 4 years of future sales) that investors are applying to the firm’s stock.

Seeking Alpha’s 1Y Advanced chart for RSG illustrates the massive performance of the company’s stock over the past year with shares appreciating almost 25% and outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) by over 4%.

While such returns are impressive, it is important to note that earnings have only grown 14% in the same period.

In order to complement these relative valuation methods, I made a DCF model for RSG stock where I aimed to find the current value of the sum of RSG’s future cash flows by discounting them against my time value of money.

I got all of the FCF data and balance sheet items from Seeking Alpha’s Financials tab for RSG and calculated a 6.8% YoY FCF growth rate over the past decade. I think this is a fair estimate for Republic Services and therefore rounded the value up to 7% in order to avoid being speciously accurate.

The terminal growth rate of 3% is roughly inline with my expectations for long-term inflation in the U.S. and the 7.98% discount rate reflects the annualised return produced by the S&P 500 over the last 93 years. I use this rate to create a valuation which includes my opportunity cost for investing in RSG instead of putting my money in the SPY index fund.

Utilising these numbers, I came to a fair value estimate of just $128 per share or an overvaluation of almost 80%. Such a massive difference between the market value and the value of all future cash flows of the business illustrates the premium attached to current prices.

With this in mind, I suspect that in the short-term (1-12 months) Republic Services stock may face some downward pressure especially if quarterly results come in softer than anticipated. The reality is that all of the growth in 2025, 2026 and 2027 is already incorporated into the stock price and I suspect a more bearish macroeconomic outlook could result in a selloff in RSG.

In the long-term (1-10 years), I believe that the firm will continue doing good business and extracting real value from shares. Nevertheless, I wonder for how long investors may be willing to pay such a premium for the stock and remain cautious about the impact such an overvaluation may have on returns over the coming decade.

Republic Services Risk Profile

I really only see two key threats: market cyclicality and regulatory compliance.

Industries, households and businesses will tend to produce slightly less waste during times of economic downturns due to the more limited spending and consumption that occurs. These customers would also be more likely to seek better deals for waste services in such times which could place some margin and growth pressures on Republic Services.

Still, I believe the necessity of waste management companies helps insulate Republic Services from a portion of the volatility associated with macroeconomic downturns as at the end of the day, the trash still needs to be collected.

I also see Republic facing regulatory risks which could arise from non-compliance with local or national guidelines. While this threat is quite small and the regulation of waste management businesses is already hugely advanced, sudden changes or new requirements could see Republic either facing fines or the requirement for costly investments in order to remain compliant.

This could harm their operational profile and potentially reduce margins or stress their balance sheets should capital requirements or penalties be significant.

Nevertheless, I still consider the firm to face a limited amount of risks with the company being quite well equipped to deal with these challenges at present time.

Of course, these matters are highly subjective and I urge all investors to complete their own research into these topics should they be of concern to you.

Summary

Republic Services is a great example of a highly profitable business with a wide and robust economic moat that helps protect their outsized earnings from competitive degradation.

A leading position within the waste management industry combined with an efficient operational structure, highly valuable assets and a healthy balance sheet are just some of the many advantages enjoyed by the firm.

Last year’s results support these claims with the firm producing a great set of earnings results despite a macroeconomic backdrop that has seen many industrial firms struggle.

My admiration for the enterprise run by Republic Services is real and the company is (as a business) one I would love to buy and hold forever.

Still, I've found it hard to look past the significant overvaluation that seems to accompany shares at present time. There is simply no room baked-in for underperformance by the company even despite their robust business economics.

With this in mind, I must award a Hold rating for the stock as investing at these levels means already paying for almost four-year’s worth of earnings which is simply excessive in my opinion, even for a great company like Republic Services.

data.title: Columbia Capital Allocation Portfolios Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Global equity and bond markets generated negative returns in the fourth quarter of 2024, with the U.S. equity market the exception. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) and the Russell 3000 Index rose 2.41% and 2.63%, respectively. In a reversal from the prior quarter, large-cap equities led the way within the U.S. equity market, and growth-oriented stocks outpaced value-oriented stocks across the capitalization spectrum.

Internationally, the MSCI EAFE Index, measuring developed market equities, returned -8.11%, and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, measuring emerging markets equities, returned -8.01%. Currency moves had a significant impact on returns. As for fixed-income markets, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned -3.06%. Reversing their gains from the prior quarter, long-duration U.S. Treasuries struggled, with the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Index returning -9.42%. In contrast, short-duration U.S. Treasuries remained in positive territory, with the FTSE Three-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index up 1.23%. Among corporate bonds, the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index eked out a 0.17% gain, but the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Bond Index returned -3.04%. Securitized assets performed similarly to investment-grade corporate bonds, per the -3.01% return of the Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Index. Emerging markets debt, per the Bloomberg Emerging Markets USD Aggregate Index, returned -1.47%. The global fixed-income market overall, as measured by the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index, returned -5.10%. Commodities returned -0.45%, as measured by the Bloomberg Commodity Index.

Positioning

Given the negative returns in the global markets broadly, absolute returns in all five portfolios of the Columbia Capital Allocation Series were negative. On a relative basis, all five underperformed their respective blended benchmark, as style decisions detracted most across all five portfolios, generally driven by regional country tactical views within international equities. Asset allocation decisions also dampened relative results across all five portfolios. Underlying fund manager performance detracted in the two most conservative portfolios but added value in the three more aggressive portfolios.

Across all five portfolios, overweight allocations to and underlying fund manager performance within U.S. large- and mid-cap equities and U.S. small-cap equities contributed positively to relative results. Underlying fund manager performance also proved effective within international equities allocations across all five portfolios, more than offsetting the detracting effect of country-level views within the three more aggressive portfolios but not enough to do so in the two more conservative portfolios. Fixed-income allocations, especially to core fixed-income and higher-quality mortgage-backed securities, detracted most from all five portfolios’ relative results, largely driven by underlying fund manager performance. The portfolios’ slight overweight to high-yield bonds contributed positively. Across all five portfolios, a moderate use of leverage detracted from relative results, given the negative absolute returns in global equities and core fixed income, and active currency positioning detracted from relative results.

During the quarter, we generally maintained the portfolios’ equity and fixed-income allocations. Within equities, we continued to favor U.S. equities over international equities and emerging markets equities over developed international equities. Within the U.S. equity market, we maintained neutral positioning on style preferences, including large- versus small-cap equities as well as growth versus value. On the fixed income side, we held our preference for sectors, such as investment-grade debt, which should benefit both from a renewed strategic interest in duration assets and from a favorable corporate environment for risky assets. Tighter spreads in lower-quality markets, such as high yield and emerging markets debt, kept us from overweighting those sectors, though we believe they remained attractive on a more strategic basis. We saw an improved picture for Treasury inflation-protected securities, consistent with renewed concerns about inflation and maintained an overweight funded out of Treasuries in all portfolios.

Outlook

At the end of the fourth quarter, we believed the backdrop remained conducive to positive equity returns, and we believed fixed-income markets were fairly priced. While the equity rally has been sustained for some time, we do not anticipate a derailing catalyst in the near term. That said, we acknowledge U.S. large-cap equity valuations remain stretched, and this prevents us from a larger portfolio overweight to equity for now. There is heightened uncertainty regarding Federal Reserve policy given renewed inflation concerns for the year ahead. However, in our view, yields were fairly valued at year-end 2024, and we believe policymakers are unlikely to surprise markets regarding the magnitude of upcoming changes to interest rates. From a diversification standpoint, we remain encouraged by correlations between stocks and bonds, which have remained negative in recent quarters, suggesting a return to form for U.S. Treasuries as a flight-to-quality asset and a valuable diversifier in multi-asset portfolios.

Fund performance

Columbia Bond Fund Institutional Class (MUTF:UMMGX) shares returned -3.28% for the period ended December 31, 2024. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned -3.06% for the same period.

Market overview

Bonds across the quality and maturity spectrum posted negative returns for the fourth quarter of 2024, driven by a significant rise in Treasury yields. As the period opened, the market was anticipating that the U.S. Federal Reserve was on course to steadily ease monetary conditions following September’s initial 50 basis-point cut to the federal funds target rate (a basis point is 1/100 of a percent). However, the outcome of the November presidential election raised the prospect of policies viewed as likely to increase inflation, including higher tariffs and more deficit-funded spending. While the Fed went on to cut interest rate twice more by year-end, these upside risks to inflation caused the Fed Committee to signal its expectation for a shallower, more gradual easing cycle in 2025.

Against this backdrop, the bellwether 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 80 basis points to end December at 4.57%. Finally, credit-risk premiums tightened despite an exceptionally rich starting point as markets embraced expectations for deregulation, more tax cuts and animal spirits that could fuel further strength in Corporate America.

As gauged by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, the broad U.S. investment-grade taxable bond market returned -3.06% for the quarter. Within the Index, U.S. Treasuries registered a loss of -3.14% (Bloomberg Treasury Index) with longer maturities most impacted, as yields moved higher across the maturity spectrum. Corporate bonds modestly outperformed duration-neutral Treasuries but still registered a loss for the period. Agency mortgage-backed securities lagged other index components, with returns hamstrung by the combination of higher Treasury yields and interest rate volatility.

Lower-quality sectors largely shrugged off lower Treasury prices to generate positive total returns, with leveraged loans besting their fixed-rate counterparts.

Performance summary

The Columbia Bond Fund (Institutional Class shares) returned -3.28% during the quarter (after expenses), underperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg Barclays U.S. Aggregate Index, by 22 basis points. Contributors and detractors from performance included:

Duration: The fund’s longer-than-benchmark duration positioning detracted from relative results as Treasury yields moved higher across maturities.

Sector Allocation: Sector allocation decisions contributed to relative performance, with gains largely stemming from an out-of-index allocation to non-agency mortgage-backed securities and overweight allocation to asset-backed securities. In contrast, an overweight allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities and underweight in investment-grade corporate bonds detracted from relative results.

Security Selection: Security selection detracted from relative results, with the relative underperformance concentrated in the agency mortgage-backed security sector. This result was partially offset by favorable security selection in CMBS and investment-grade corporates.

Outlook and positioning

Having made meaningful progress in reducing inflation, the Fed reduced the overnight lending rate by a cumulative 100 bps over the final months of the year intending to mitigate weakness in the labor market. With the labor market stabilizing, the Fed’s attention has shifted back to inflation. The U.S. economy continues to grow above trend, which increases the risk that inflation could remain sticky above the Fed’s 2% target.

Compounding this risk are the incoming Trump administration’s proposals on trade, taxation and immigration. While details remain scarce, the addition of this uncertainty will likely manifest in greater caution and a higher threshold to move the easing cycle forward.

We believe the Fed’s approach to monetary policy in 2025 will be dictated by two emerging risks. The first represents the tail risk of the economy overheating, fueled by a negative supply shock from tariffs and mass deportations, plus a positive demand shock from tax cuts and higher fiscal spending. This combination could impede further progress on reducing inflation and bring the Fed’s easing cycle to an abrupt end. In contrast, the other risk constitutes a continuation of labor market trends that have been in motion for over two years. That is, that wages remain a weak passthrough into inflation, which provides confidence that it will not run away to the upside. With inflation under control, the Fed can focus on adjusting policy to better support the slow but steady decline in the labor market.

The former scenario has dominated market sentiment in the early days of 2025 and intermediate- and longer-maturity Treasury yields have risen uncomfortably close to cycle highs. Investors appear to be extrapolating above-trend growth well into the future without any risk to further downside in the labor market. While neither feels appropriate, we are hesitant make a high-conviction bet on either of the above-mentioned scenarios given the overhang of so much policy uncertainty. That said, real yields, or yields after accounting for expected inflation, have risen to a level that have historically rewarded investors for owning duration over cash. As such, we are maintaining exposure to the belly of the Treasury yield curve where we can capture attractive term premium while remaining somewhat insulated from volatility associated with the threat of higher fiscal deficits. Credit-risk premiums have tightened to new post-Financial Crisis lows, which creates asymmetric risk of a move wider. Our positioning reflects a desire to participate in the story of elevated yields while also mitigating potential downside from spreads widening. To do this, we’re focused on higher-quality opportunities in shorter maturities where prices historically demonstrate less volatility.

data.title: Trivago: Inadequate Risk/Reward Ratio
data.description:
Introduction

It has been about a year since my last article on Trivago (NASDAQ:TRVG), in which I was trying to figure out whether this company might represent a “Cigar-Butt” opportunity. After I wrote the article, the company's valuation dropped by one-third (coming to equal cash on hand) and then quickly rose again in the last period to about +50% over the price a year ago. Have the company's fundamentals changed or have they remained the same? With this second article I want to try to answer this question. To avoid unnecessary repetition, in case you are not familiar with Trivago's business model I invite you to read my previous article.

Financial Performance

The most recent information is from February 4, the day the company released its Q4 2024 results. The results were positive overall, Q4 FY24 was the first quarter with positive growth after more than 1 year of decline (more precisely 6 quarters).

In the last quarter, revenues grew 3% to €94.8 million, with Referral Revenue up 5% (€93.5 million). Net income was €5.1 million (+104% yoy), and Adjusted EBITDA was €11.1 million, up 52% yoy. On the strategic side, on the other hand, a new marketing campaign was launched in key markets such as US, UK and Canada, starring Trivago's new ambassador Jürgen Klopp. The results achieved exceeded analysts' and management's expectations and could represent the beginning of a possible turnaround.

The results for January 2025 are even more promising. Management reported that in January all three segments reported “Strong double-digit” revenue growth, and they believe the company is well positioned for the full 2025, expecting at least high single-digit growth over 2025 and to reach at least breakeven in terms of Adjusted EBITDA. From a balance sheet perspective, the company continues to be in a very good position with €130 million in cash and no long-term debt.

Management also added that the challenges in the performance marketing channel that had reduced margins last year have been reduced, thereby also reducing the volatility of future earnings. Finally, the intention to prioritize marketing investments in the short term, reinvesting profits to sustain momentum, was confirmed.

Trivago divides its operations into three geographic segments: Americas, Developed Europe, and Rest of World. Although globally performance was significantly better than in previous quarters, not all three geographies performed equally well.

Developed Europe represented the weakest segment of the three, with revenues down 2% from last year and a significant reduction in marketing expenses (-15% yoy) that supported a marked improvement in ROAS, which stood at 176% (compared to 152.7% in Q4 2023). The main reason for this negative performance is the decline in monetization on the platform. The other two segments performed significantly better, particularly Rest of World. Revenues in this segment in Q4 increased by +15% yoy and expenses in marketing by only +3%, also increasing ROAS here to 148.3% (134.5% in 2023). Monetization in this segment was also slightly weaker than last year, but the increase in traffic from brand investments and the improvement in booking conversion offset this downside.

To conclude, the Americas segment's revenues grew by 8% yoy at the expense of +18% yoy growth in marketing expenses, thus causing ROAS to decline, which stood at 159.6%, down sharply from 173.4% in Q4 2023. Branding activities helped increase traffic and thus revenues, but since these are investments with a medium to long-term time horizon, the increase in marketing expenses did not lead to a commensurate increase in revenues.

Overall then, advertising spending remained stable (with variations in different geographies as we have seen) and ROAS globally increased to 162.9% (155.4% in Q4 2023) especially due to efficiencies in Developed Europe and Rest of World.

Valuation

In my previous article on Trivago to try to motivate the very low multiples compared to EV I wrote:

The market penalizes the fact that the company was unable to invest the cash it generated. The special dividend distribution proved that management does not know how to manage and invest the company's resources profitably. There may also be fears that planned advertising investments will fail to generate sufficient ROAS, and may consequently be a way to burn cash. Thus, the market may take market capitalization rather than enterprise value more into consideration when determining multiples.

At the time of writing this article Trivago's market capitalization is $271 million and EV is about $173 million. As mentioned earlier in 2025 the company expects to reach at least breakeven in terms of Adj. EBITDA, this implies negative EBIT and Net Income. There are not many analysts who have provided their estimates for the years after 2025, this is clearly a sign of the uncertainty and difficulty in predicting the future of this company.

I personally do not expect a big improvement in profitability before 2027. As stated by management, investments in branding take time to generate returns, and it is their intention to reinvest the profits generated to increase spending on marketing. From a purely mathematical point of view, if we were to do a DCF, this strategy would not reward the company's valuation, as you move positive cash flows further out over time and the effect of the discount rate is felt more. From a strategic point of view, however, I think it is the correct choice, since you are trying to give the company a future rather than focusing on earning as much as possible today while jeopardizing the company's ability to generate revenue in the future.

Although from the results of the last quarter the situation of Trivago seems to be improving, the uncertainty and difficulty in estimating future results is still as high as it was a year ago. Also in the previous article I stated:

For a company with no competitive advantage and not so clear growth potential, a P/EBIT multiple between 8 and 10 might be right.

I still don't think Trivago has a particular competitive advantage, so I still think the multiples I used last time are valid. However, if Trivago were able to enter a new phase of revenue growth, the market might reward it with higher multiples. I decided to proceed with an evaluation using the multiples methodology.

Best Case:

  • Revenue 6% CAGR until 2029 (€617 million FY29)
  • 6% EBIT margin (€37 million FY29)
  • X13 EV/EBIT --> EV = €481 million ($504 million)

Base Case:

  • Revenue 4% CAGR until 2029 (€ 560 million FY29)
  • % EBIT margin (€22,4 million FY29)
  • X10 EV/EBIT --> EV = €224 million ($235 million)

Using a discount rate of 12%, the present value of the EV is:

  • Best Case: Present Value of EV = €306 million + Net Cash: €94 million -> Market Cap. = €400 million ($419 million)
  • Base Case: Present Value of EV = €142 million + Net Cash: €94 million -> Market Cap. = €236 million ($247 million)

Based on this quick assessment Trivago would still appear overvalued for the Base Case. The assumptions I have made in the Best Case are deliberately optimistic. I do not think it is very likely that Trivago can manage to easily grow at 8 percent annually through 2029 and achieve an EBIT margin of 6 percent (especially taking into account past performance).

Conclusion

What had prompted me to write the first article on Trivago was the extremely low valuation, making it a “Cigar-Butt” and where the company's liquidity acted as a good margin of safety, offering an opportunity to ride the upside in the event of improvement in the company's fundamentals and to stem losses in the event of failure. At the current price, I don't believe that Trivago can offer an adequate risk/reward ratio. Thus, to answer the initial question, I do not believe that Trivago's fundamentals have increased sufficiently to justify this valuation. If we add to the high uncertainty about Trivago's ability to generate substantial cash flows, the absence of a real competitive advantage and an unattractive valuation, here we get as a result a highly speculative investment.

data.title: Genpact: AI Explosion Exposure, But Not That Cheap Anymore (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Genpact Limited (NYSE:G) appears to be delivering outstanding quarterly results thanks to its exposition to the demand of AI algorithms. Already serving a significant amount of clients from very different industries, Genpact's business model is well diversified, which brings significant protection in case of economic recession. The company also delivered significant free cash flow growth and revenue growth in the past as mentioned in my previous article about the stock. Right now, I do not think the shares are cheap anymore, but I would follow the company carefully because the business appears very solid.

Genpact: Cash In Hand, Growing Target Markets, And Proprietary Technologies

Genpact presents itself as a professional services and solutions firm with over 125k employees and operations in around 30 countries. With clients from the Fortune Global 500, the company offers digital operations services and expertise in data, technology, and AI.

Clients receive proprietary solutions based on the company's AI-based platform Genpact Cora. Genpact Cora brings automation, analytics, and AI technologies that clients can use to improve customer experience, and accelerate digital transformation. The following lines have been taken from the most recent annual report.

It draws insights from our deep domain and operations expertise in our target industries and service lines to create analytics-based solutions that are focused on improving customer and user experience to accelerate clients’ digital transformations. Source: 10-k

In my view, Genpact is a massive corporation with an established list of clients, and a lot of cash to make investments in marketing. In 2024, the company reported $648 million in cash, and $4.9 billion in total assets. In the last quarter, the total amount of liabilities stood at $2.59 billion.

Besides, the company is targeting markets that are expected to grow at a massive rate in the future. The global digital transformation market is expected to grow at close to 27% CAGR from 2024 to 2030. Besides, the AI market alone is expected to grow at close to 19% CAGR from 2025 to 2034.

The global digital transformation market size was estimated at USD 880.28 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate CAGR of 27.6% from 2024 to 2030. Source: Digital Transformation Market Size And Share Report, 2030

The global artificial intelligence market size is calculated at USD 757.58 billion in 2025 and is forecasted to reach around USD 3,680.47 billion by 2034, accelerating at a CAGR of 19.20% from 2025 to 2034. Source: Artificial Intelligence Market Size to Hit USD 3,680.47 Bn by 2034

It is also worth noting that Genpact's business model appears quite diversified because it serves different industries including financial services, consumer, healthcare, high tech, and manufacturing. Hence, given the level of diversification, in my view, economic recessions may not impact the company's financials that much.

Beneficial Revenue Growth Driven By Innovation In Data-Tech-AI

The company delivered beneficial figures in the last quarter including quarterly revenue growth of 9%, with Data-Tech-AI up by 12%. Data-Tech-AI services include cloud-based software-as-a-service offerings in combination with AI technologies, data and advanced analytics.

In 2024, total revenue stood at 6.5%, with 10% EPS growth. The new numbers are pretty much in line with the expectations that I expressed in my previous article about the company. I invite readers to have a look at the information and stock catalysts shown in my previous piece.

Given the outlook for 2025 and the comments in the most recent press release, I would be expecting further revenue growth and free cash flow growth driven by investments in AI and Data. In this regard, the company provided the following information in the last business communication.

Looking ahead, we are incredibly excited about the future as we accelerate the pace of innovation and change at Genpact. Building on our industry-specific domain expertise, our investment in Data, AI and Agentic Solutions is positioning us as a clear leader in AI-driven transformation and driving superior value for our clients. Source: Quarterly Press Release

The outlook for 2025 includes revenue close to $5.029 billion and $5.125 billion. With these new figures and the recent stock price dynamics, in my opinion, it is the right time to review my financial model, and offer a new valuation opinion.

The Stock Repurchase Program And Recent Dividend Increases Could Accelerate The Demand For The Stock

In the last quarterly press release, the company noted an 11% increase in the dividend rate. The company also approved a new repurchase program of $500 million. In my view, it is clear that Genpact is trying to take care of shareholders. As soon as new investors have a look at the company's stock repurchases and dividend distribution, future demand for the stock could accelerate. In this regard, the company provided the following information in the last quarterly press release.

Genpact's Board of Directors declared a quarterly cash dividend for the first quarter of 2025 of $0.17 per common share, an 11% increase, payable on March 26, 2025 to shareholders of record as of the close of business on March 11, 2025, and approved a $500 million increase to the Company's existing share repurchase authorization. The newly approved quarterly dividend represents a planned annual dividend of $0.68 per common share, increased from $0.61 per common share in 2024. Source: Quarterly Press Release

My Financial Expectations, Net Debt Decline, And Price Target Of $55-$56 Per Share

Considering the most recent results delivered for the year 2024 and the guidance for 2025, I changed a bit my expectations about the future. In the last report, the company reported FCF of $481 million. FCF stood at $305 million in 2023, $427 million in 2022, and $287 million in 2015. Besides, net debt decreased from $914 million in 2023 to $756 million in 2024.

My free cash flow expectations would include 2025 FCF of $525 million, with 2028 FCF of $686 million, 2030 FCF of $810 million, and 2031 FCF of $950 million. If we use cost of capital of about 7.3% and discount future free cash flows from 2025 to 2031, the NPV would stand at $3.6 billion. Now, with a terminal multiple of 12x and net debt of $756 million, the implied equity valuation would be $9.8 billion. Finally, we would be talking about a fair price of about $55-$56 per share. Given the current stock price, the upside potential is limited.

  • NPV of Free Cash Flow @ WACC Of 7.3%: $3,651.09 million
  • NPV of Terminal Value @ 12x FCF: $6,961.56 million
  • Enterprise Value: $10,612.65 million
  • Net Debt: $756.00 million
  • Equity: $9,856.65 million
  • Shares Outstanding: 179.183 million
  • Target Price: $55-$56 million

Competitors trade at 21x earnings, 11x-12x FWD EBITDA, and 1.98x FWD sales. The company trades at close to 17x FWD earnings, and 11x FWD EBITDA. I think that the upside potential appears limited. Hence, I decided to close my position in the stock.

Risks From Lower Than Expected Revenue Growth From AI

In my opinion, the stock price recently increased because many investors did learn about the company's AI services. Given that Genpact is offering a number of services around the new AI boom, many analysts may be incorporating significant revenue growth expectations. Revenue increased from $4.3 billion in 2022 to $4.76 billion in 2024.

In my view, in the future, revenue growth from AI services may be lower than expected. In that case scenario, future free cash flow expectations could decline, and the demand for the stock would decline. In sum, we could see a decrease in the stock valuation.

Risks From Failed AI Development And Flawed AI Algorithms

Genpact could suffer from failed development of AI technologies. Algorithms could deliver wrong information to clients, or outputs may not be as efficient as expected. According to Genpact, certain AI algorithms could also disclose sensitive information of clients, or break intellectual property rights. If clients decide to sue Genpact because of these failed results, I would be expecting fines. If the net income declines because of damages payable by Genpact, the valuation of the stock could fall.

The development, adoption, and use of AI technologies are still in their early stages. AI algorithms may be flawed, and datasets may be insufficient or contain biased information, which could result in unexpected, low quality or otherwise inadequate outputs. Ineffective or inadequate AI development or deployment practices by us, our clients, or third parties with whom we do business could result in unintended consequences, such as disclosure of sensitive information, infringement of third-party intellectual property rights or other incidents that impair the acceptance of AI solutions or cause harm to individuals or society. Source: 10-k

Risks From Lack Of Qualified Personnel And Changes In The Labor Conditions

In the coming years, I would be expecting a number of new laws about immigration and new labor law regulations. In my opinion, the business model of Genpact is based on hiring qualified personnel in key jurisdictions, where the company signs agreements with clients. If management cannot find an adequate number of job candidates, or salaries are too expensive, I would expect declines in productivity and revenue growth. I think that Genpact may see free cash flow margin declines.

Risks From Data Regulations In The U.S., Europe, The UK, And Other Jurisdictions

Genpact has to respect a number of data regulations not only in the United States, but also in the European Union and other jurisdictions. In addition, in the last annual report, the company noted that according to recent decisions from some courts in the United States, it is now easier for consumers to sue companies for data breaches. With this in mind, data breaches could bring a significant number of problems to Genpact. In my view, if the company needs to pay more to protect the way data is processed, future free cash flow margins could decline.

All fifty U.S. states and the District of Columbia have implemented separate data security breach notification laws with which we must comply, and some states have added specific data security standards to their existing laws. Some courts have become more willing to allow individuals to pursue claims in data breach cases, indicating that it may become easier for consumers to sue companies for data breaches. Related laws and regulations govern our direct marketing activities and our use of personal information for direct marketing, including the Telemarketing and Consumer Fraud and Abuse Prevention Act, Telemarketing Sales Rule, Telephone Consumer Protection Act and rules promulgated by the Federal Communications Commission, and CAN-SPAM Act. Source: 10-k

Conclusion

Genpact operates in high growth markets, and is offering services to a diversified list of clients in different industries. With exposure to the recent explosion of demand for AI algorithms, the company appears to be delivering beneficial quarterly reports that include substantial revenue growth and free cash flow. In addition, the recent dividend increase and recent stock repurchase program announced will most likely accelerate the interest for the stock, which may also accelerate stock demand. With all that being said and considering my free cash flow forecasts and risks, I cannot say anymore that the company is cheap. In my view, the margin of safety recently decreased substantially. Hence, I decided to close my position in the stock.

data.title: Celsius: Game Changer
data.description:
Celsius Holdings, Inc. (CELH) just ended a horrible year where the stock crashed to multi-year lows. In a surprise move, the energy drink company made a game-changing acquisition of a leading female-focused functional wellness drink brand. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bullish on the stock due to the valuation of the deal and the return of the business to growth.

Alani Nu Deal

Prior to releasing Q4 '24 results, Celsius announced a deal to acquire Alani Nu for $1.8 billion in cash and stock. The Alani Nu brand was a big part of why Celsius failed to grow during 2024.

According to the data, Alani Nu sales soared to $0.6 billion last year and produced an adjusted EBITDA of $137 million. Celsius only paid 3x last year sales for the business.

The deal is broken out to $1.275 billion in cash and the issuance of 22.5 million shares for $500 million. The net purchase price is only $1.65 billion after including a $150 million net tax benefit.

In the 4-week period thru February 8, Alani Nu had grown volumes by a reported 63% with price gains, while Celsius has struggled to even grow volumes. The deal allows Celsius to consolidate leadership in the healthy energy drink category.

The combined company will control ~16% of the energy drink market. Red Bull and Monster both control ~30% of the market, suggesting Celsius plus Alani has the potential to double in size to match the market leaders.

The deal offers about $50 million in synergies, but the investment story is all about consolidating leadership in the healthy energy segment. Not to mention, the opportunity to expand sales and grow both brands internationally.

Turnaround Q4

Celsius finally reported a solid quarter with Q4 results. The company beat consensus analyst estimates for the December quarter as follows:

The energy drink company reported a $5.2 million revenue beat, but the numbers were still down over 4% from last year. The PepsiCo (PEP) inventory de-stocking as the main domestic distributor has made the financials difficult to analyze.

On the Q4 '24 earnings call, CFO Jarrod Langhans confirmed the quarterly inventory reset had impacted revenues by $8+ million as follows:

So, it was between $8 million and $10 million in terms of the impact.

In essence, Celsius would've reported small revenue growth in the quarter, with the actual revenues closer to $340 million. Still, the energy drink company had a tough quarter.

The domestic revenues were considerably weak, with sales down 6% to $312 million. International revenues grew 39% to $20 million to offset the domestic weakness, even when factoring in the Pepsi inventory issues.

Even before this Alani Nu deal rally, Celsius had fallen to an exceptionally cheap level. The energy drink company was struggling to grow, and Alani Nu was a big reason why.

The market cap is now ~$7.5 billion, with sales targets at $1.6 billion for 2025. The company had a solid balance sheet with nearly $900 million in cash prior to the deal, leading to the valuation slumping to below 4x EV/S targets, while Monster Beverage (MNST) held a valuation of 6x.

The Alani Nu deal was an incredible bargain at less than 3x TTM sales. Even just using the $2 billion revenue target from 2024, the stock trades at a market cap just above $8 billion, with the balance sheet switching to a small net debt balance.

The new Celsius has a far better growth profile now, making the stock a bargain trading at a valuation discount to Monster. Back in the growth phase, Celsius regularly traded at 8x forward EV/S targets.

With the momentum of Alani Nu, normalized Pepsi inventory entering 2025 and the international opportunity, Celsius should report far higher growth in 2025. Even just 20% growth is $2.4 billion in revenue, placing the stock at closer to 3.5x sales targets.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Celsius got a lifeline by acquiring Alani Nu at such a cheap price via limited stock dilution. The energy drink company is now set up for a big 2025 and the stock is still relatively cheap even after the 2x% post deal rally.

Investors should continue buying shares here above $30.
